title: 2025 07 24 Petitions
displayTitle: 2025-07-24 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Sam Fisher: Inquiry into threats and violence against women MPs and local authority reps
topic: Petition of Sam Fisher: Inquiry into threats and violence against women MPs and local authority reps
description: Thursday, 24 July 2025 | Petitions Committee Petition of Sam Fisher: Inquiry into threats and violence against women MPs and local authority reps Sam Fisher Parliamentary Service Rafael Gonzalez-Montero, Chief Executive Bridget Wilson, Security Manager - Systems & Enablement Dr Justin Barry-Walsh
descriptionSummary: Petition of Sam Fisher: Inquiry into threats and violence against women MPs and local authority reps. Sam Fisher. Parliamentary Service

Right here. Do you want to No, that's fine. You're welcome to say that. Yeah, let's do this. If you, if you so want, you can take your submission as read. Yes. So, yeah, leave as much time as you possible if you can for, for questions. Yeah. So I came at this because I worked in parliament.

I worked in the beehive for four years. I kept coming across this. I have a number of friends who are women who will not stand for office because of the hate and vitriol they will receive. Mm-hmm. I also know some very brave women. Look, if I was a woman, I wouldn't stand simple as that. It just isn't worth it. Um, the mayor of Christchurch has actually suffered some quite bad, um, attacks by which were settled in court.

Um, and I just feel there's something wrong, and it would be very nice for us to actually try and understand it, to see if we can do something about it. I think my experience of women in politics has been, they're often the ones who are the voice of reason, because I'm all tied up in my ego and things. Now at the same time, you know, women aren't necessarily the best.

It depends, but I think we are actually cutting ourselves off badly if we remove a large proportion of our population from actually wanting to, to hold office. Mm-hmm. Um, the perspectives that women bring are different, you know, I think it's important we hear that. And I suspect my, my picking up is that I have two or three very good friends who would've been very good representatives in some form, but won't stand either local body or at national level.

Um, it appears to me we've got ourselves locked into certain things which we don't think we can change. And I'm thinking here about, um, it's just not worth standing. We can't shut up the haters. We can't do that. And I think there's got to be more than that. I mean, it seems to me that we, we could actually start to sort of push up the people who are standing, the one who are standing, and any group, and just actually support them in some way by saying this will actually provide these views to the community.

I think there should be a proactive, something around that mm-hmm. Around just actually explaining why it's so important. Mm-hmm. Um, as a white male in my fifties, it's all, you know, I totally get, I've had a lot of things happen for me, which are really good. I've stood for office and I've been, um, abused quite a bit too.

So, but women, I saw some of the stuff that went to Sandra Lee, uh, to Lala Hare and to the various ministers in the Alliance government. And some of it was disgusting. Absolutely disgusting. I don't know how people get up in the mornings and they can write that s**t. You know, it's that sort of thing. Yeah. Yeah.

So it occurred to me a review or an inquiry into what we can do to actually make it safer to get more candidates. Instead, I think it would actually spill out more than just women. I think it would spill out further than that. It might make the way clearer for everybody of different communities to stand for election. Um, and I think there might be some things we can find out. There's some very good statistics out there about the sort of people who've been, um, creating the abuse.

I remember seeing a spreadsheet of the ones that were coming into the Alliance offices. These are the, these are our regular customers. So we do have lists of people and lists of things. We just need to find a way, I think, to try and neutralize that nasty pressure. I mean, that's pretty much my, my only argument. It seems to me, if we're gonna be having inquiries, this would be a good one.

Because frankly, if we're not being represented at the highest level and at local level by good candidates, we are suffering. Mm-hmm. And I think, I think O New Zealand deserves better than that. Um, yeah, that's pretty much all I have to say, unless we wanna deal with anything more specific. I talked a bit about some of the things I had seen, some of the research I've seen, but I don't wanna waste your time too much.

And I thought, it's just not good enough. So that was where it came from. Mm-hmm. I would just like to see an inquiry into why this is happening, why we allow it to happen, and what can we done to support women in politics and other groups in politics as well, to actually do what we want them to do, which to represent us.

It was about that as, as we used to say in sports, playing the man, but not quite the opposite, really playing the woman. And I just think that's part of the problem. I suspect it's something about schools and, and those sorts of things in terms of encouraging women for being seen as people who can lead and can provide information for others.

I think one of the things I saw, I remember one particular email to Sandra Lee, which I think security were investigating, and it was just disgusting. It was just awful. I think we still have a, we are still people who believe in helping each other at base. We still believe in a strong community. And I think, uh, reading this petition, reading, uh, your petition when you were giving this evidence, I thought, you know, it is not okay to become desensitized to it as well. Mm-hmm. I've had, um, my car vandalized many times.

Um, and I've had someone walk up to me in the car, um, you know, saying all sorts of things. Um, yeah. Come up to my house saying all sorts of things. Shouldn't be happening. No, we're not that sort of country. We shouldn't be that sort, But yeah. Well said. Well said. Thank you. Hey, thank you, Sam. Right, Ref. Thank you, Mr. Chair. And, um, I read Sam's petition, uh, very carefully, and I absolutely and wholeheartedly believe with what he's written in that submission.

Um, I think it is two parts to it. The first one is, is, is, is the need to do an inquiry. And I think that's, uh, is, it will be a huge piece of work because a lot of the things that come online, we actually don't know where they come from. They are, they are trolls. Um, there are people that just make it their life to just write things.

And they are sometimes, uh, faceless, um, faceless people that you don't know who they are. Mm-hmm. From our part, the important thing is what, uh, both, uh, Paolo and the Honorable Janssen, he were saying it's a matter of not being desensitized by it.

And one of the problems for the service is the lack of reporting. Um, and I'm always happy, we're always happy to work with the parties and with the members if there's a much easier way, uh, for this to be reported. But it should never be allowed. It should never be accepted.

Um, and if we have, uh, the ability to look at the size of the problem, which we know is really big, we just can't show you evidence of how big it is. Um, it will make it much easier for us to think of, uh, different solutions to, to this problem. But I mentioned last week in the, in the panel that if this happened in any workplace, it wouldn't be allowed.

People will be in very big trouble. And, and I think nobody will stand for it. So I don't think that members should stand for it either. Um, so we do need to work closely. Another comment I've made, made to parties is that it is really important that members, I know members are busy, time poor as you were saying, but it is important that members familiarize themselves with the different tools they have at their disposal to, um, make sure that they are protected.

And if, if sometimes, uh, for systems fail, and if sometimes a member doesn't get contacted for a reason, do ask someone, uh, whether it is, uh, uh, uh, uh, another colleague, whether it is your relationship manager, because those are things are easy to, uh, fix.

And, and systems are easy to fix. The thing that we can't fix is someone getting hurt. Um, and at the end of the day, they may get hurt and someone will say, well, it was because the system failed, but by then it's too late because the person's already heard. Um, we are working really hard on finding solutions to this problem.

We've been exploring, uh, tools, uh, that can be used for several, uh, purposes. One could be filtering out, um, from members, um, work accounts of, uh, social media. Uh, it could be things from filtering out, um, abusive language so that they don't have to see it down to using a tool that would capture those comments and then flag them in a different way for us to take, uh, more action.

So those are the sort of things we are exploring. We are also exploring, uh, the different tools available in legislation to see if there's anything that, uh, uh, could be used in the future or could be used much better to make sure that, um, that we can take a more proactive approach on behalf of members.

So those are the types of discussions we are having at the moment. Very encouraging. Thank you. Yeah. There's never been an inquiry this time before, has there? No, no. This has been quite, uh, it's not new. Yeah. And it's not a new problem. It's not exclusive to New Zealand. It's been happening everywhere. Yes.

Yeah. Correct. Those are the two groups we know, uh, are the, the ones that are most affected by it. Yeah. Um, so I guess that will be a really good start. Yeah. Because that's the accounts we hear from members, um, uh, female members here and across the world, and, uh, members with from ethnic minorities.

Yeah. Uh, those are the ones that get targeted the most. Yeah. Yeah. Okay. Appreciative. You have anything you'd want to add? Um, I think I just echo risk comments. It's the information, the connection with members to get information, timely information to enable us to act when we need to.

And not after the fact. Because a lot of the time, the answers that we're getting is, you know, two weeks after it's happened, which makes it harder to kind of backtrack and piece together evidence, um, and support is, you need support as these things occur. Yeah. Um, I do, I agree with wrap that focusing on those two areas should be the priority and then by, you know, a connection that will flow into the wider members or getting approved support.

But I started with women because I just had that experience and I thought maybe this might cross the line.

We've got got 10 minutes. Alright. Look. Yeah. Okay. So the things I, I would emphasize firstly is it very clearly is a problem. We know that, um, we have research to back that up. And of course I was at your panel discussion last week as well. It was, and that's another thing I'd say, I appreciate that Parliament recognizes that as problem.

I know from briefing the speaker, he's concerned about the area and I find it very encouraging to see both Ray and Bridget here as well. So yeah, I would acknowledge that aspect as well. Uh, the other things I would just state is that the harm is real. Um, and it is not confined to physical violence.

We always worry about that. But I've learned in my work with the Fixated Threat Assessment Center, that we actually need to think a little bit wider than that in terms of harm. Physical violence is a rare event, uh, but psychological distress changes in behavior, uh, and this, uh, effect on, uh, engagement with the community, uh, is well described in the research.

We know from our conversations with those affected that it does impact. So it's broader than that. Um, the only other two things I'd highlight is, we know from using the same study design first and Westminster in 2009, and then, uh, through to parliament here via Norway and Queensland, that there's been a marked rise in harassment via social media, which is utterly no surprise, it's a recent phenomenon.

We're still getting to understand that. And I'd just like to, again, endorse, I was a little bit concerned to hear that an MP might have their car vandalized to be approached in public Yeah. And not be reported. Um, our, our services rely on reporting. It's really important, we've learned from ftac that it's really important for us to try and get in front of people as often as possible to talk as much as possible about what we do, what our particular niche is.

Um, and so that's an important part. And when every time we do that, we do see a spike in, uh, reporting afterwards. I'll leave it there. Thank you. Questions. Alright. But I noticed that there's been a lot that's escalated in the last wee while, whereas the tipping point where we, we, um, we take everything seriously. Like the question. I know, but Yeah, it certainly is. Um, look, so many different answers spring to my mind.

The, the, the first one is that we like to hear about things early in our service, which to be clear focuses on the fixated individuals. Our focus is on prevention.

For some time. You know, come on. Gosh. So look at, I, I just don't think I can properly answer that. And I'll, I'll probably just talk too much of if I try to more. Mm-hmm. Yeah. Mm-hmm. You have anything? Hmm. See, processing? Um, yeah. I don't drive a marked car. I don't wear branding if I'm alone. Mm. I don't go into events at night where I'm branded. I'm happily a no one back venture that no one really recognizes.

Scary. And so I'm on, and then she was, he was on my wife's side, and he was screaming so close to the glass that the glass was getting his, his his spittle and my wife. So my wife's upset. Um, and then I'm, I'm thinking I should go down and talk to this guy.

Um, but the fact he knows where you live too, I have moved house. Yeah. Literally. Um, may I comment? I, I two comments? So the first one is actually a slightly more general, uh, comment. As I said, we are agnostic as to whether an inquiry is a good idea or not. Mm-hmm. Um, and there's been discussion that it should be focused on woman and on ethnicity.

I have no issue with that. But where it occurs, whether it's male or female mm-hmm. It's still a problem and it still creates harm as well. Our research shows there's a gendered effect, but that's not dismissed to dismiss that it can happen no to male as well as female, uh, mps public figures.

Second comment I'd make is, um, uh, what, six years and, well, eight years really. Here in Parliament, which, um, it's amazing that stalking isn't a criminal offense in New Zealand, but after we pass this law, it will be.

And I think that, um, that gives, um, parliament another, um, avenue to go down. And of course, being the Greens, we are always about prevention. So we want some rehabilitation put in there as well. Um, before becoming an mp, I'm a first term mp. I was very naive to, uh, some of the security issues, um, that all of us, not just people in the public eye should be aware of.

And one of the things that, um, security do here, they look after us very well, is we have this app and, um, which sort of yeah. Keeps us, keeps us safe. They were looking into different, um, parliaments and different security measures that they take, um, in different parliaments. And because of this exact issue, the rise in really scary stuff happening, particularly for women, um, parliamentarians. And then you add on, on top of that things like women of color and all the, all the rest of it.

Uh, and they actually were really, really interested in this. So some of the things that our parliament is doing to keep us safe, um, may actually be implemented in other, um, countries around the world. But, um, yeah. Thank you. So I just wanted to, to let you know that we're really appreciate that and we really appreciate the work that our security do here as well. Thank you so much.

Yeah, you are welcome. Thank you very much. Thank you, Sam. Thank you very much. We, we, we really appreciate all the work that all of you do as well. And the last thing I'd say is, I think New Zealand is better than this. I think Tel is a better country. We just have to tap into that, that identity question of are we better people? Then? I think we are. That's my, that's my hope.
